skill.name: cavecrew
skill.description: Decision guide for choosing compressed cavecrew subagents for code lookup, small edits, and focused review tasks.
skill.summary: Choose compact subagent workflows for investigation, small edits, and diff review.
skill.author: juliusbrussee

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported command execution, path traversal, and weak cryptography signals. Manual review found these are false positives caused by Markdown code spans, relative documentation links, and frontmatter text. No runnable code, network access, prompt injection, or data exfiltration evidence was found.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Command Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The external command alerts point to Markdown inline code and fenced output contracts. They name subagents, tools, and example output formats, but they do not execute shell, Ruby, or other commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are Markdown documentation and examples only. No executable file, command runner, or user-controlled shell interpolation is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Markdown Links Misclassified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The path traversal alerts are README links to related repository documentation. They are not file read or write operations, and they do not instruct the model to access secrets or hidden paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is limited to static Markdown links using relative paths. There is no filesystem API, command invocation, or data access instruction attached to those links.

low_findings.title: Frontmatter Text Misclassified as Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptography alert points to the skill description frontmatter. The file does not define or request any cryptographic algorithm, hashing routine, credential handling, or encryption behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 starts a YAML description block and contains no cryptographic API or algorithm name. The surrounding context is skill metadata.

content.value_statement: Long coding sessions lose context when subagents return verbose prose. Cavecrew helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code choose compact subagent workflows for lookup, small edits, and review.
actual_capabilities: Recommends when to use cavecrew-investigator for read-only code location tasks.
Recommends cavecrew-builder for small edits limited to one or two files.
Recommends cavecrew-reviewer for compact diff or file review findings.
Compares cavecrew subagents with vanilla Explore and Code Reviewer options.
Defines terse output contracts for investigation, edit verification, and review results.
Describes chaining patterns for locate, fix, and verify workflows.
limitations: It is a decision guide, not a standalone command runner.
It does not provide the referenced subagent implementations inside this skill folder.
It is designed for coding workflows, not general writing or research tasks.
Its compact output style can be terse for direct human reading.

use_cases.title: Find Code References Quickly
use_cases.description: Use the investigator path when a developer needs definitions, callers, or usages returned in compact path-first form.

use_cases.title: Handle Small Scoped Fixes
use_cases.description: Use the builder path when a maintainer already knows the target files and wants a constrained one or two file edit.

use_cases.title: Review Diffs Without Long Prose
use_cases.description: Use the reviewer path when a team wants prioritized findings instead of broad architecture commentary.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Delegation Path
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for help selecting the right cavecrew agent.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cavecrew to decide whether this task should stay in the main thread or go to an investigator, builder, or reviewer.

prompt_templates.title: Locate a Symbol
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cavecrew-investigator to find where this symbol is defined and where it is called. Return compact path-first results.

prompt_templates.title: Patch Known Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cavecrew-builder for this one or two file change. Verify the edited locations and report only the compact change summary.

prompt_templates.title: Run Locate Fix Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced chained workflow for a contained bug fix.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use cavecrew to locate the relevant sites, apply a limited fix only if scope stays under two files, then review the diff.

output_examples.input: Find where the payment status is calculated and summarize the relevant call sites.
output_examples.output: A compact list of matching file locations, symbols, and short notes, followed by a total count.

output_examples.input: Make this known two-file validation fix using cavecrew-builder.
output_examples.output: A brief change summary for each edited location, followed by a verification status.

output_examples.input: Review the current diff with cavecrew-reviewer.
output_examples.output: A sorted list of findings with severity labels and concise fixes, or a clear no issues result.

best_practices: Use investigator first when the target file or symbol is not already known.
Use builder only for obvious one or two file edits with a narrow scope.
Use vanilla review tools when you need detailed rationale or architecture feedback.
anti_patterns: Do not use builder for broad features, large refactors, or unclear file targets.
Do not ask reviewer for general impressions when you need full prose analysis.
Do not send compact cavecrew output directly to nontechnical readers without explanation.

faq.question: What does Cavecrew do?
faq.answer: It helps an AI coding assistant choose compact subagent workflows for code lookup, small edits, and review.

faq.question: Does it run commands?
faq.answer: No. The reviewed files are documentation and decision guidance, not executable scripts.

faq.question: When should I use the investigator?
faq.answer: Use it when you need definitions, callers, usages, or other code locations in a compact format.

faq.question: When should I avoid the builder?
faq.answer: Avoid it when the change spans three or more files, has unclear scope, or needs design exploration first.

faq.question: How is this different from Explore?
faq.answer: Cavecrew favors compressed, structured output. Explore is better when you need prose, rationale, or architecture commentary.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. Manual review found no executable code, network activity, prompt injection, or data exfiltration behavior.
